summary: I joined the Seattle Labor Chorus about four or five months before I gave a talk at their annual Thanksgiving celebration. The chorus taught me to sing and gave me a sense of community and empowerment that I hadn't experienced before. I struggled at first with learning my alto part, but the support from members like Barb Powers and Celia Matson helped me grow. Singing with the chorus connected me deeply to labor struggles and inspired me to participate in union events and performances.

I grew up hearing University of Wisconsin pep rally songs on family trips to my grandma's house at Thanksgiving. Being from Madison, I was thrilled to hear Lou Truskoff rewrite lyrics about Governor Scott Walker and union busting in Wisconsin at a conference in Seabeck, Washington. My connection to the university is part of my background and informs my understanding of labor struggles.
cues: University of Wisconsin pep rally songs

summary: For over a dozen years, my Girl Scout leader taught me that different girls could sing different words and rhythms simultaneously, which I later learned was called singing in parts. This early experience with singing shaped my musical understanding even though I didn't formally learn the term until seventh grade chorus. The Girl Scouts were an important part of my early musical education.

Because my daughters were deaf and I wanted them to experience music, I signed them up for Suzuki cello lessons for three years. This method gave them a way to engage with music despite their hearing challenges. It also motivated me to interpret for musicians like Pete Seeger and John McCutcheon at Quaker gatherings so my girls could know song.

I had the opportunity to interpret for Pete Seeger at Quaker gatherings so my deaf daughters could experience the songs. His music, along with others like John McCutcheon, was part of the cultural and political music I engaged with after moving to Seattle. Interpreting for him was a meaningful way to connect my family to music despite hearing challenges.

Alongside Pete Seeger, I interpreted for John McCutcheon at Quaker gatherings to help my deaf daughters experience music. His performances were part of the political and folk music scene I connected with after moving to Seattle. This work was important to me as a mother wanting to share music with my children.

I moved to Seattle about six years before 2011, bringing with me only a dozen CDs mostly of political musicians and the Dixie Chicks. Seattle became the place where I joined the Seattle Labor Chorus and found a community that empowered me to sing and engage in labor activism. The city is central to my musical and social transformation.

Sometimes when I drove solo through the Cascades, I would sing loudly and happily, trying to catch harmonies fluttering like butterflies. These drives were moments of personal joy and musical exploration before I fully embraced singing with the chorus. The Cascades hold a special place in my memory as a space of musical freedom.

In February 2011, I lobbied in Olympia with a Quaker group and heard the Peace Chorus there. A woman signed all the songs, which sparked a new idea for me about combining music and signing. Olympia was a key place where my journey toward joining the Seattle Labor Chorus and deeper musical involvement began.

I attended the Fellowship of Reconciliation conference in July 2011 at Seabeck, Washington, where I took a song workshop led by Lou Truskoff. The conference connected me with people like Barb Powers and deepened my involvement in music and activism. Later, I sang songs at FOR events in honor of Jim Roe and with D'vorah, showing how important this organization has been in my life.

Seabeck, Washington was the site of the Fellowship of Reconciliation conference I attended on July 4, 2011. There, I participated in a song workshop that introduced me to new songs about union struggles and connected me with people who would become important in my singing journey. Seabeck holds a special place as the starting point of my deeper musical activism.

Lou Truskoff facilitated a song workshop at the Fellowship of Reconciliation conference in Seabeck in 2011. He had rewritten lyrics about Governor Scott Walker and union busting in Wisconsin, which thrilled me as someone from Madison. Later, I led songs with lyrics by Lou Truskoff on picket lines, showing how his work influenced my activism and singing.

Scott Walker was the governor of Wisconsin whose union busting policies inspired rewritten song lyrics by Lou Truskoff at the 2011 FOR conference. As a Madison native, I was thrilled to hear these songs and later led them on picket lines in Seattle. His actions were a catalyst for much of the labor activism and music I engaged with.

Barb Powers was the woman who offered me a ride to my first Seattle Labor Chorus rehearsal after the FOR conference in 2011. I sat beside her and became an alto in the chorus, and her friendship and support were crucial as I learned to sing. Barb was part of the welcoming community that made me feel included and empowered.

VOX is the system we use in the Seattle Labor Chorus to listen to our music parts at home. I used VOX tapes to practice my alto part week after week, though I struggled to hold onto it at first. VOX was an important learning tool that helped me improve my singing and feel connected to the chorus outside rehearsals.

Celia Matson was a friend who gave me rides to and from Seattle Labor Chorus rehearsals. I loved her friendship and would ask her many questions about music and singing during the drives. She was part of the supportive community that helped me feel included and learn the craft.

Janet Stecher was our chorus coach who used corporate sign language during rehearsals, which felt like home to me because it is my family's language. I loved watching her direct and teach us, and I wrote down her advice on how to sing and live. Janet also sent me lyrics that I sang with friends during difficult times, and we sang together at FOR events honoring Jim Roe.

Janet Stecher once told us to 'sing like Perry Como,' meaning to sing with confidence and not to whine. This advice stuck with me as I learned to sing with the Seattle Labor Chorus and face my fears about performing. Perry Como became a symbol of the style and attitude I aspired to in my singing.

Thanksgiving was a special time in my childhood when my family sang songs like 'Over the River and Through the Woods' on the way to my grandma's house. Years later, I gave a talk at the Seattle Labor Chorus Thanksgiving celebration, reflecting on how singing had changed my life. Thanksgiving marks both my early musical memories and my adult musical journey.

Two years ago, I flew to Texas to visit my friend Judith in hospice. Judith had spent years organizing delegations to the border to meet women in the colectivos and learn about their lives and work. Singing songs with her and others in that room was a powerful moment of connection and honoring her life's work.

Judith was a dear friend who spent much of her life organizing delegations to the border to meet women in the colectivos. I visited her in hospice in Texas and sang songs with her and others to honor her work and legacy. She has been an example of deep friendship and commitment to social justice that inspires me.

The colectivos are groups of women at the border whose dignity and work Judith helped us understand through delegations she organized. Meeting them was part of Judith's important work, which I honored by singing songs with her in hospice. The colectivos represent the deep cross-cultural friendships and social justice efforts that inspire me.

Jim Roe was honored at the Fellowship of Reconciliation conference where Janet and I sang the same song in his memory. His work and legacy have been important to our community and inspired many of the songs we sing. Remembering him through music connects me to the broader labor and peace movements.

My first performance with the Seattle Labor Chorus was at a Service Employees International Union conference. I was nervous and unsure about what to wear, but once on stage, I connected deeply with the audience and the songs. The SEIU event was a milestone that marked my growth as a singer and activist.

The day after the SEIU conference, I was at Harborview Medical Center and asked a nurse if she had been at the event. When she said yes and mentioned loving the song 'We Were There,' I hugged her, something I hadn't done the night before. Harborview became a place where the connection between music and community felt real and tangible to me.

After a rehearsal one winter, I was driving home merging onto I-5 when a song came to me with words about singing the hardest songs and hope and courage. This moment on I-5 inspired me to write lyrics that reflect the spirit of the Seattle Labor Chorus and the ongoing work of labor activism. The highway became a place of creative inspiration for me.

Joe Hill is a symbol for us in the Seattle Labor Chorus, and I wrote lyrics inspired by his legacy about how the work isn't done and how many chords form a part. Singing about Joe Hill connects me to the history of labor struggles and the ongoing fight for justice. His spirit motivates me to keep singing and standing with workers.
